rows.plain: Prevent exposed closures from touching any surface that is not clean while they are being positioned. This helps keep the product protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Exposed closures contacting a non-clean surface during positioning can add bioburden or recontaminate; relevant to load integrity with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled loading areas reduce risk but manual handling makes incidental contact a recurring possibility. Careful positioning to avoid non-clean contact adds handling time. Avoiding contact relies on operator technique and spatial care. Contact with non-clean surfaces can waste exposed closures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between critical parameter readings and the cycle phase
rows.plain: Make sure each important reading matches the stage of the process when it was taken. This helps people understand whether the process is performing as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between parameter readings and cycle phase can misinterpret whether exposure occurred; relevant to valid monitoring with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Automated cycle sequencing keeps parameters aligned with phase reliably on validated systems. Phase-aligned readings are displayed live with no time cost. Judging whether readings fit the phase needs some process understanding. Misalignment can indicate cycle faults that waste loads.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among critical parameter readings within the same cycle interval
rows.plain: Make sure important measurements taken during the same production period do not conflict with one another. This helps show that the process is behaving as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among critical parameter readings within a cycle undermines confidence in delivered conditions; central to valid monitoring with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated redundant sensors normally agree closely, and cross-checking is routine, so significant disagreement is uncommon. Reading agreement across sensors is instantaneous on displays. Spotting disagreement needs some operator attention but systems flag it. Sensor disagreement can trigger costly investigations or reruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the cycle with the chamber door unsecured
rows.plain: Do not start the cycle until the enclosed area’s door is fully secured. This helps protect people, materials, and the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Initiating with an unsecured door is a safety and cycle-integrity failure; relevant and severe for personnel but usually interlocked and self-evident.
rows.sat_rat: Physical door interlocks are near-universal and reliably prevent cycle start with an unsecured door. Door interlock check is instantaneous and does not delay the start. Door securing requires no special skill as interlocks enforce it. An unsecured door start is prevented by hardware, avoiding damage or loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that documented evidence fails to demonstrate the defined SAL for representative closure loads under saturated steam
rows.plain: Make sure the records and test results clearly show that representative closed loads reach the required level of sterility when treated with steam. This provides evidence that the recommended process works.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to document evidence of SAL is central to demonstrating the job was done; consequences are compliance failure and inability to defend product sterility, though the physical sterilization may still be adequate.
rows.sat_rat: Mature GMP validation practice makes documented SAL demonstration for representative loads highly dependable, with rare residual gaps in representativeness. Standard steam-sterilization validation protocols (IQ/OQ/PQ with BIs and thermocouples) are well established and executed on defined timelines. Validation engineers and microbiologists routinely design and interpret SAL demonstrations using recognized guidance, though it demands specialized expertise. Validation studies are costly but budgeted as a normal part of autoclave qualification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of utility-borne contaminants contacting closure loads during sterilization
rows.plain: Prevent unwanted material carried by the equipment's water, air, or other services from touching container closures during cleaning or sterilizing. This helps keep the closures safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Utility-borne contaminants contacting loads could recontaminate or compromise closures; relevant to protected sterility with severe potential though comparatively uncommon.
rows.sat_rat: Established clean-utility standards substantially control utility-borne contamination, though quality drift can still occur. Clean-steam and filtered-utility provisioning is designed in early, adding little operational delay. Pharmaceutical-grade utility design is a mature specialty with established standards. Clean-steam generators and filtration add significant capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of removing the closure load before safe-opening conditions are confirmed
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of removing the processed load before it is confirmed safe to open. This helps prevent exposure to heat, pressure, or other hazards.
rows.imp_rat: Removing the load before safe-opening conditions is confirmed is a personnel-safety and integrity concern; relevant to safe operation with severe injury potential but tangential to sterility outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure/temperature interlocks on the door reliably prevent opening before safe conditions on modern autoclaves. Safe-opening interlocks may delay opening until conditions are met. Interlocks remove most operator judgement about safe conditions. Preventing unsafe opening avoids injury cost and load loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury from residual pressure, steam, or hot surfaces during removal
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that someone is hurt by trapped pressure, hot steam, or hot surfaces when removing a processed load. This helps make removal safer.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury from residual pressure/steam/hot surfaces is a serious safety hazard but peripheral to the sterilization CFJ itself; high severity when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks, PPE and cool-down procedures make injury from residual heat/steam uncommon though not eliminated. Cool-down waits before removal cost time but protect personnel. Safe handling requires trained use of PPE and awareness, a modest skill demand. Injuries cause downtime and liability cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of including closures with unverified washed or siliconized status in a load
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of putting caps or stoppers into a load when it has not been confirmed that they were properly washed and coated. This helps ensure the items are ready for processing.
rows.imp_rat: Including closures with unverified wash/silicone status affects input state and bioburden assumptions; relevant to load integrity with moderate-to-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record gating, status labels and hold controls are mature practices that make inclusion of unverified closures uncommon though not impossible. Verifying wash/silicone status via batch records adds a quick check before staging. Status verification is a standard clerical/label check requiring modest attention rather than deep expertise. Catching unverified closures upstream avoids costly wasted sterilization cycles.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between validated-state documents and approved autoclave operating conditions
rows.plain: Keep the approved operating conditions for the steam-sterilizing equipment consistent with the documents that define how it must be operated. This helps ensure the equipment remains approved for use.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between validated-state documents and approved operating conditions is largely a documentation/control gap; relevant to compliance with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and periodic review keep documents aligned reasonably well, though drift between updates is a known recurring issue. Reconciling validated-state documents with operating conditions is a periodic review, not a per-cycle delay. Detecting divergence between documentation and actual conditions demands document-control and validation expertise. Document drift rarely wastes material directly but risks costly compliance findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closures damaged or degraded during sterilization
rows.plain: Prevent caps or seals from being damaged or weakened during the germ-killing treatment. This helps keep the containers properly closed and the product protected.
rows.imp_rat: Closures damaged/degraded during sterilization is a product-integrity concern that can affect container closure function; relevant but secondary to the sterilization CFJ, with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cycles are validated to protect elastomer integrity, so damage is infrequent though extended or repeated cycles can still degrade closures. Damage from cycles is not a timing issue for the overseer. Selecting closure-safe cycle parameters is a validation task, minimizing routine judgement. Damaged closures cause scrap and rework cost, which validated cycles largely avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of closures that lose dimensional or functional integrity under the specified cycle limits
rows.plain: Keep caps or seals from losing their shape or ability to work during the specified treatment. This helps them continue to protect the product afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Closures losing dimensional/functional integrity under cycle limits affects container closure integrity; relevant to product quality but secondary to the sterilization CFJ, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closure material compatibility with steam limits is fairly well understood, making integrity loss uncommon within validated limits. Integrity testing after cycle limits adds characterization time. Assessing closure dimensional/functional integrity under heat needs materials and container-closure expertise. Damaged closures represent direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of processed closures with unacceptable residual moisture at removal
rows.plain: Make sure processed closures are not too wet when they are removed. Too much leftover moisture can indicate that processing was not completed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Unacceptable residual moisture at removal is a post-cycle quality attribute affecting downstream use; relevant but secondary to achieving SAL, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Post-vacuum drying phases control closure moisture fairly well but residual moisture remains a common recurring complaint for closures. Drying phases and post-vacuum extend cycle time to control moisture. Achieving acceptable moisture is cycle-design driven, low operator skill. Excess residual moisture can force reprocessing, wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure packages with residual water exceeding the specified acceptance limit
rows.plain: Reduce how often packages containing closures have more leftover water than allowed. This helps ensure the packages meet the required loading condition.
rows.imp_rat: Residual water above limit affects downstream drying/use and can harbor issues, but is largely a post-cycle quality attribute peripheral to achieving SAL; moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Post-wash draining and drying steps are standard but residual water in crevices or packages still slips through inconsistent visual checks. Draining and inspecting for residual water adds handling time before wrapping. Judging acceptable residual moisture is partly subjective and operator-dependent. Residual water can defeat sterilization and cause rework, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual moisture retained by closures at completion of the specified cycle
rows.plain: Remove as much leftover water as possible from the stoppers or lids by the end of the cleaning cycle. This helps keep the containers properly prepared.
rows.imp_rat: Residual moisture at cycle completion is primarily a drying/quality attribute for downstream handling; relevant but peripheral to SAL achievement, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Residual moisture on closures is a common, only-partly-solved nuisance since closure geometry traps water despite drying phases. Achieving dryness adds drying-phase time to the cycle. Specifying effective post-cycle drying for closures requires vacuum-drying know-how. Extended drying and vacuum consume energy and cycle time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that claimed throughput lacks evidence for worst-case closure loads achieving the defined SAL
rows.plain: Claims about how much the equipment can handle should be supported by tests showing it works even with the hardest loads to close while still reaching the required level of germ reduction. This helps ensure the stated capacity is realistic.
rows.imp_rat: Throughput claims lacking worst-case SAL evidence are primarily a validation/marketing gap; relevant to defensibility but lower direct severity to a given sterilized load.
rows.sat_rat: Claimed throughput is frequently stated for ideal rather than worst-case SAL-achieving loads, so evidence gaps recur during procurement. Worst-case load studies add time but are part of established PQ campaigns. Defining and justifying worst-case closure loads for throughput claims requires seasoned judgement that is not always applied by vendors. Vendor throughput claims are often offered without the cost of worst-case validation being borne upfront.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required steam, water, drainage, electrical, and compressed-air parameters missing or unverified
rows.plain: Make sure all needed requirements for steam, water, drains, electricity, and compressed air are listed and checked. This helps confirm that the equipment can work properly at the site.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/unverified utility parameters (steam, water, drainage, electrical, air) is largely a commissioning/readiness gap; relevant to reliable operation but moderate severity for a given load.
rows.sat_rat: Utility requirement checklists exist but parameters are frequently left unverified against actual site conditions, causing recurring surprises. Compiling and verifying all utility parameters is time-consuming during specification. Cross-referencing steam, water, drainage, electrical, and air parameters requires multidisciplinary utility engineering skill. Missing parameters discovered late trigger costly rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of closure loads requiring repeat sterilization or disposal after failing to achieve the defined SAL
rows.plain: Reduce how often a load of closures must be sterilized again or thrown away because it was not made sufficiently free of harmful germs. This lowers waste and operating costs.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat sterilization or disposal after SAL failure is primarily rework/waste; the sterility failure is caught, so severity is operational rather than patient-safety catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Well-validated saturated-steam cycles make load failures uncommon, though load-configuration errors still cause occasional reprocessing. Failed loads requiring reprocessing consume schedule time, but validated cycles keep occurrences low. Once cycles are validated, routine operation needs modest skill to avoid failures. Repeat sterilization and disposal waste material and energy when they occur.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unsubstantiated closure sterility entering fill-finish is attributed to the overseer's governance
rows.plain: There should be solid proof that the caps or stoppers entering the filling process are free of germs. Without that proof, the person responsible for oversight could be blamed for an unsupported claim of cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of unsubstantiated sterility to the overseer's governance is a governance/accountability framing; peripheral to the physical CFJ though reputationally consequential.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that usable closure throughput falls below projected peak demand while achieving the defined SAL
rows.plain: There should be enough usable, properly protected caps or stoppers to meet the busiest expected demand while reaching the required safety level. A system that cannot keep up could delay production or create shortages.
rows.imp_rat: Throughput below peak demand while achieving SAL is a capacity/business concern, not a sterility failure; peripheral to the CFJ with moderate operational severity.
rows.sat_rat: Usable throughput at the defined SAL is often overestimated relative to real peak demand, a recurring gap in capacity planning. Peak-demand throughput shortfalls surface late, often only after cycle timing is measured in operation. Reconciling usable throughput at SAL against peak demand requires cross-functional modelling skill that is unevenly available. Underprovisioned capacity forces costly overtime or added equipment later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a critical cycle excursion during completion review
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a critical cycle excursion at review risks releasing under-sterilized closures, yet ranking holds it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated data review and alarm flags make overlooked excursions uncommon, though manual record review can still miss subtle events. Completion review adds a defined but short review step. Spotting a critical excursion in the record demands trained review judgement. Overlooking an excursion risks releasing a bad load, a serious cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cycle alarms or interruptions left unresolved during completion review
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved cycle alarms at completion review risk accepting a bad cycle, but ordering caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm logs and mandatory disposition steps make leaving alarms unresolved uncommon in disciplined records. Resolving alarms before sign-off adds review time proportional to alarm count. Resolving alarms requires trained interpretation of their significance. Unresolved alarms can indicate compromised, wasted loads.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the actual load configuration and the recorded validated configuration
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of actual vs recorded validated configuration matters for traceability but is a comparatively administrative discrepancy; kept at the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented load configurations help but real-time verification of actual vs recorded is manual and error-prone. Comparing actual to recorded configuration is a brief verification. It relies on operator care in matching the physical load to records. Deviation from validated configuration risks an invalid, wasted cycle.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of dispositioning an affected closure load without complete abnormal-condition evidence
rows.imp_rat: Dispositioning without complete abnormal-condition evidence risks releasing suspect loads, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation procedures require evidence but completeness for abnormal events is inconsistently captured in practice. Assembling complete evidence before disposition adds review time. Judging evidence completeness requires QA/deviation expertise. Dispositioning without evidence risks costly wrong release or scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the load shifting from the validated position before cycle start
rows.imp_rat: Load shifting from validated position before start can impair penetration, but ranking caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Racks and short load-to-start intervals limit shifting, but there is no positive verification, leaving some risk. Confirming the load hasn't shifted is a brief pre-start glance. Detecting shift relies on operator observation. A shifted load can under-process, wasting closures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the load configuration and validated cycle bounds
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between load configuration and validated cycle bounds is relevant to lethality assurance, but ranked below batch 1; capped, so relevance 4 and severity 3 keep the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validated load patterns and load sheets constrain this but adherence is largely procedural and manually checked. Checking load against validated bounds is a quick pre-start step. It requires operator knowledge of the validated load envelope. An out-of-bounds load can be under-sterilized and wasted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of verified cycle settings changing before cycle start
rows.imp_rat: Verified settings changing before cycle start could invalidate the run, but ordering forces the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe locking and access control make settings changing after verification rare on modern systems. Change-lock between verification and start is enforced instantly. Little skill needed as system locks parameters after verification. An undetected change before start risks a wasted cycle.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of using an obsolete or mismatched specification for the closure load
rows.imp_rat: Using an obsolete/mismatched load specification can drive a wrong cycle, but ranking holds it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems and effective-date checks make mismatched specs uncommon though not impossible. Retrieving the current specification adds a brief verification step. Recognizing an obsolete spec depends on document-control awareness. Using a wrong spec can ruin a load, an expensive but infrequent error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure packages with damaged or incompletely secured barriers after assembly
rows.imp_rat: Damaged or incompletely secured barriers risk recontamination, but this ranks below batch 1; both proxies at 3.
rows.sat_rat: Seal checks and sealing equipment are established, but incomplete or damaged seals still recur due to manual sealing variability. Verifying seal integrity after assembly is a quick routine check. Confirming secure barriers is a learnable inspection task with moderate attention demands. Incompletely secured barriers cause rework and occasional scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing particulate or microbial contamination during closure-load assembly
rows.imp_rat: Introducing particulate/microbial contamination during assembly adds bioburden, but batch ordering caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled environments and gowning make gross contamination rare, though low-level particulate/microbial ingress during assembly is never fully eliminated. Cleanroom gowning and controlled handling add time but are built into the workflow. Contamination-avoiding technique requires trained aseptic discipline and constant vigilance. Contamination events cause costly investigations and lost loads.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prepared closures acquiring microbial contamination while awaiting sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Prepared closures acquiring contamination while awaiting sterilization raises bioburden, but ordering holds at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Time limits and controlled staging areas help but hold-time excursions still allow occasional microbial acquisition. Prompt processing after prep limits exposure but depends on scheduling. Minimizing microbial pickup relies on controlled environment discipline. Bioburden growth risks failed loads and investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prepared-closure packages with breached protective barriers before sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Breached protective barriers before sterilization create contamination risk, yet ordering places this below batch 1; capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Visual barrier checks are standard practice yet small breaches often go undetected until later, leaving recurring failures. Inspecting barriers before sterilization is a brief added step. Spotting breached barriers requires attentive visual inspection that varies by operator. Breached barriers force repackaging or scrap, a modest material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of staged closure loads with wrapping or containment damaged during positioning
rows.imp_rat: Wrapping/containment damaged during positioning creates a recontamination pathway, but ranking caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Protective containers and careful handling keep positioning damage relatively rare. Inspecting wrapping after positioning adds a brief check. Detecting positioning damage is routine visual inspection. Damaged containment causes repackaging and delay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of staging a closure load for an incompatible autoclave cycle
rows.imp_rat: Staging a load for an incompatible cycle risks under-processing, but ranking holds the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cycle-specific labeling and scheduling controls make incompatible staging uncommon. Confirming cycle compatibility is a quick check against documentation. Matching load to cycle relies on operator reading the correct spec, a modest skill. Wrong-cycle staging risks a fully wasted or failed load.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of error in recorded exposure values used to judge sterilization-cycle acceptability
rows.imp_rat: Error in recorded exposure values used to judge acceptability could wrongly pass a cycle, but ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic data recording with validated instruments makes recorded-value errors uncommon and mostly auto-detected. Recorded exposure values are captured automatically with no manual delay. Automated data capture removes most manual transcription skill needs. Recording errors are cheap to prevent electronically and rarely cause waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterilization-impacting change is misclassified as having no validated-state impact
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying a change as no-impact undermines validated state; serious but ranked below batch 1, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control forms and SME review reduce misclassification, but it remains a persistent judgement-dependent vulnerability. Change classification happens at assessment time and rarely creates timing pressure. Correctly judging whether a change impacts the validated state is a demanding expert-judgement task where misclassification is a well-known human-error mode. A misclassified change can trigger expensive rework or recalls if impact is missed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of validated-state changes implemented before impact approval
rows.imp_rat: Implementing validated-state changes before impact approval risks uncontrolled cycles, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control workflows enforce pre-approval fairly well, though informal or urgent changes sometimes bypass sequence. Impact approval before implementation adds lead time to changes. Enforcing pre-implementation approval relies on change-control discipline more than deep skill. Premature changes can force costly rework or invalidation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a closure lot affected by an unresolved change enters sterile filling before validated-state disposition
rows.imp_rat: A change-affected lot entering sterile filling is a serious contamination pathway, but batch ordering forces the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine and change-control linkage make premature entry of affected lots uncommon when systems are followed. Quarantine holds pending validated-state disposition delay downstream filling but are enforced. Deciding whether a change affects a lot's sterility basis needs cross-functional expertise. Holding affected lots incurs carrying cost yet prevents costlier non-conforming use.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected validated-state elements omitted from a change assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting affected validated-state elements from a change assessment leaves gaps in control, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists help, but omissions of affected elements remain a recognized weakness of change assessments. Assessment completeness is set at authoring time, not a recurring delay. Identifying every affected validated-state element in a change requires broad system knowledge and is a frequent gap in assessments. Omitted elements cause rework or escaped defects, an intermittent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that interactions among concurrent or cumulative changes affecting sterilization conditions are overlooked
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking interactions among concurrent changes can defeat assurance, but ranking holds the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard change control handles changes individually and poorly captures interactions, making this a real recurring blind spot. Evaluating change interactions is done at assessment, not per cycle. Recognizing cumulative or concurrent change interactions is among the hardest expert-judgement tasks and is often missed. Overlooked interactions can trigger large, costly failures downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an external sterilization-impacting change is not reported before use
rows.imp_rat: An external impacting change unreported before use undermines validated state, but ordering caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier change-notification agreements exist but are imperfectly honored, leaving a notable gap. External-change reporting timing depends on suppliers and is not internally controlled. Recognizing an external change as sterilization-impacting requires supplier-quality awareness. Unreported external changes can cause expensive undetected failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between post-change sterilization conditions and the approved validated state
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between post-change conditions and validated state threatens control, but ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and requalification practices generally catch post-change discrepancies, with residual gaps for subtle drift. Post-change verification against the validated state adds project time, not routine cycle time. Confirming post-change conditions match the approved state requires validation engineering judgement. Re-validation after changes is resource-intensive but bounded to change events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical cycle value is accepted from an instrument outside calibration status
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a value from an out-of-calibration instrument undermines the acceptance decision; capped at 3.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration programs with due-date tracking make acceptance of values from uncalibrated instruments rare. Calibration status is checked at review, adding little time. Calibration tracking is procedural and needs limited specialist skill day-to-day. Using an out-of-cal instrument can invalidate batches at high cost, which drives the controls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that sterilization deviations remain unresolved while the autoclave continues operating
rows.imp_rat: Continuing operation with unresolved deviations risks releasing suspect loads, but ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deviations are tracked but often run past target closure dates, so lingering open items are a persistent problem. This need is explicitly about how long deviations stay open while the autoclave keeps running, and investigations routinely drag on. Timely closure depends on scarce investigator and SME availability, a common bottleneck. Prolonged open deviations tie up product and staff, accumulating carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical cycle values lack valid calibration status during processed-load review
rows.imp_rat: Critical values lacking calibration status at review invalidate release evidence, but ordering caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration due-date systems make lapsed-status values at review rare when maintained. Calibration status is confirmed during processed-load review with minimal delay. Checking calibration validity at review is a routine, low-skill verification. Detecting lapsed calibration at review prevents expensive downstream release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of autoclave changes lacking documented impact assessment and approval before use
rows.imp_rat: Autoclave changes lacking impact assessment before use threaten validated state, but ranking holds it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems make undocumented changes uncommon in mature sites, though small changes occasionally escape. Requiring documented impact assessment before use lengthens change timelines. Producing adequate impact assessments needs qualified assessors. Undocumented changes create expensive compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterilization cycles supported by values from instruments outside verified status
rows.imp_rat: Cycles supported by unverified-instrument values undermine acceptance validity, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Verification programs make reliance on unverified instruments uncommon, similar to calibration controls. Instrument verification status is checked at review without adding much time. Verification tracking is procedural and modestly skill-dependent. Values from unverified instruments can void loads, a controlled but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of closure loads assembled outside validated loading boundaries
rows.imp_rat: Loads assembled outside validated boundaries can affect steam penetration, but this batch ranks lower; both proxies held at 3.
rows.sat_rat: Documented loading configurations and operator training keep most loads within validated boundaries, though manual loading errors still occur. Following loading diagrams adds no meaningful time. Correct loading depends on operator adherence to validated patterns, a trained but manual step. Improper loading risk wastes loads, but correct loading has negligible added cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of compliant sterilized loads rejected during disposition
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting compliant sterilized loads is a waste/yield loss, not a sterility failure; peripheral to the CFJ with low operational severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conservative disposition biases toward rejection, so compliant loads are sometimes discarded with little systematic mitigation. Re-review of unnecessarily rejected loads consumes disposition time. Distinguishing true failures from conservative over-rejection needs experienced judgement that is variable. False rejection of compliant loads directly wastes good material and reprocessing resources, and is only loosely controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of approved closure-load configurations lacking current evidence of defined SAL attainment
rows.imp_rat: Approved configs lacking current SAL evidence is a compliance gap; important but ranked lower, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Revalidation schedules cover main configurations, but less-used approved configurations can lapse on current SAL evidence. Confirming current SAL evidence per configuration is a periodic review, not a per-load delay. Tracking validation currency across configurations needs validation-lifecycle expertise that is often thinly staffed. Maintaining periodic revalidation for every configuration is resource-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of approved closure sterilization controls that do not reflect the current validated state
rows.imp_rat: Controls not reflecting current validated state weaken assurance; important but ranked lower, held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control links validation to controls reasonably, but stale procedures not reflecting the latest validated state are a recurring gap. Reflecting the validated state in controls lags because change updates take time to propagate. Keeping controls aligned to the current validated state requires change-control and validation expertise. Change management effort is a recurring administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sensor, calibration, or placement error is mistaken for inadequate closure lethality
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking sensor/placement error for inadequate lethality can misdirect diagnosis; relevant but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration programs and redundant sensors help, but sensor-versus-process confusion still recurs during deviation diagnosis. Distinguishing sensor error from real lethality failure can prolong investigations. Differentiating calibration/placement artifacts from true low lethality demands experienced metrology and investigation skill. Misattribution can trigger unnecessary batch rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that pressure-temperature evidence inconsistent with saturated steam is overlooked during deviation diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking non-saturated-steam evidence in diagnosis can mask lethality failure, yet ranking holds it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Saturation-inconsistent P-T evidence is frequently overlooked because review depends on individual analyst vigilance. Reviewing pressure-temperature saturation evidence adds analysis time in deviations. Spotting saturation inconsistencies requires steam-thermodynamics fluency that few reviewers reliably apply. Overlooked evidence risks costly wrong disposition.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that achieving the sterility assurance target causes unacceptable closure degradation
rows.imp_rat: Achieving SAL causing closure degradation is a real trade-off, but ranking places it below batch 1, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard cycle qualification includes closure integrity checks, so unacceptable degradation is usually caught before release. Assessing closure degradation against lethality requires added material testing time. Balancing SAL against closure integrity requires materials and process expertise. Degraded closures represent material waste and cost if over-processed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of routine closure loads outside the evaluated mass, density, or arrangement boundary
rows.imp_rat: Routine loads outside evaluated mass/density/arrangement bounds risk penetration failure, but ranking caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Documented load limits exist, but routine loads can drift outside evaluated boundaries without detection. Defining and monitoring load boundaries adds procedural time. Setting valid load boundaries requires process expertise. Enforcing load limits has low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified exposure parameters exceed validated autoclave capability
rows.imp_rat: Specified exposure exceeding autoclave capability means the cycle cannot deliver as defined, but ordering caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment qualification envelopes are well documented, so specifying beyond capability is rare and readily caught. Cross-document consistency checking needs meticulous coordination skill. Conflicting assumptions can hide true cost until after approval.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of closure sterilization investment-scope changes after authorization review begins
rows.imp_rat: Investment-scope changes after review begins is a stability concern peripheral to achieving SAL; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Scope changes after review begins are a recurring disruption. Scope changes during review reset the clock and add delay. Freezing scope needs disciplined change-control skill. Mid-review scope changes drive rework and added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the funding request is routed to approvers lacking sufficient authorization authority
rows.imp_rat: Funding routed to approvers lacking authority is an administrative-rework matter peripheral to the CFJ; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Delegation-of-authority matrices make correct routing fairly reliable. Misrouting to wrong approvers causes rework loops that extend timelines. Knowing the correct authority matrix is routine governance knowledge. Misrouting wastes effort but little direct money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved approval conditions blocking release of capital for the closure sterilization investment
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved approval conditions blocking capital release is a funding-process delay peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with absorbable impact (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Approval-condition tracking via stage-gate workflows is a fairly mature practice, so blockages are usually visible and eventually cleared. Unresolved approval conditions directly stall capital release and are a recurring source of delay. Clearing conditions requires coordinating multiple experts, though the executor is practiced at chasing sign-offs. Held capital carries opportunity cost but is not itself a direct spend.
